metadata.title: Why Spin-½ Exists: The Algebra Is More General Than the Sphere
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = orbital angular momentum (position space, integer only); CRIMSON = spin angular momentum (abstract space, half-integer allowed)
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no full ladder-operator derivation on screen, no SU(2)/SO(3) group theory, no Dirac equation, no explicit Pauli matrix construction. Mechanism only: single-valuedness of e^{imφ} forces integer m for orbitals; spin has no φ coordinate, so no constraint applies.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B13: palette=claude but the outro is 'OutroCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. The angular momentum algebra allows half-integer quantum numbers. Electrons in atomic orbitals cannot have them. Every electron in the universe has spin one-half. Same equations. Opposite conclusions.

card.copy: Why Spin-½ Exists: The Algebra Is More General Than the Sphere

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. The angular momentum algebra allows half-integer quantum numbers. Electrons in atomic orbitals cannot have them. Every electron in th

beats.narration_text: Start the ladder derivation. The raising and lowering operators step the projection by one unit. For that to terminate at both ends, the allowed values of the quantum number are zero, one-half, one, three-halves — every multiple of one-half is algebraically valid.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Start the ladder derivation. The raising and lowering operators step the projection by one unit. For that to terminate at both ends, the allowed values of the q

beats.narration_text: But atomic orbitals — p, d, f shells — all have integer values: zero, one, two, three. Half-integer orbitals never appear. The algebra says they should be there. The data says they aren't.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: But atomic orbitals — p, d, f shells — all have integer values: zero, one, two, three. Half-integer orbitals never appear. The algebra says they should be there

beats.narration_text: The Question: the angular momentum algebra predicts that half-integer values are valid. Every atomic orbital forbids them. Yet every electron in the universe carries spin one-half. The same commutation relations govern both. Why does the algebra give two different answers?

card.copy: Same algebra. Orbitals: integer only. Spin: half-integer. Why?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The Question: the angular momentum algebra predicts that half-integer values are valid. Every atomic orbital forbids them. Yet every electron in the universe ca

beats.narration_text: The naive reading: the ladder derivation is complete — it gives all half-integers. The algebra said so. So half-integer orbitals should exist. But they don't appear in the spectrum. The algebra must be wrong, or incomplete.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The naive reading: the ladder derivation is complete — it gives all half-integers. The algebra said so. So half-integer orbitals should exist. But they don't ap

beats.narration_text: The mechanism: orbital angular momentum lives in position space. Its z-projection eigenfunction is e to the i-m-phi. That wave function must return to its starting value after one full turn — phi plus 2-pi must give the same answer as phi. That requires e to the 2-pi-i-m equals one, which forces m to be an integer.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The mechanism: orbital angular momentum lives in position space. Its z-projection eigenfunction is e to the i-m-phi. That wave function must return to its start

beats.narration_text: Try m equals one-half. After one full turn, the wave function picks up a minus sign — it does not return to itself. A physical wave function must be single-valued. Half-integer m is incompatible with living on a sphere.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Try m equals one-half. After one full turn, the wave function picks up a minus sign — it does not return to itself. A physical wave function must be single-valu

beats.narration_text: Spin has no azimuthal angle. The spin state is a vector in an abstract two-dimensional complex space — there is no phi, no sphere, no periodicity condition to impose. The algebra's full range — including all half-integers — is realized without restriction.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Spin has no azimuthal angle. The spin state is a vector in an abstract two-dimensional complex space — there is no phi, no sphere, no periodicity condition to i

beats.narration_text: The implication: the integer restriction is a property of the sphere, not of the algebra. Angular momentum eigenstates always exist for every half-integer. Orbital angular momentum just adds a geometric boundary condition that the algebra itself never requires.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The implication: the integer restriction is a property of the sphere, not of the algebra. Angular momentum eigenstates always exist for every half-integer. Orbi

beats.narration_text: A student derives that ell equals one-half is algebraically valid and writes the wave function e to the i-phi-over-two. After one full rotation, phi goes to phi plus 2-pi and the function flips sign. She concludes ell equals one-half is forbidden for any state with a position-space wave function. Then she learns that spin one-half works perfectly, because spin has no phi coordinate to rotate.
beats.example_note: illustrative — directly from the card's example seed

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A student derives that ell equals one-half is algebraically valid and writes the wave function e to the i-phi-over-two. After one full rotation, phi goes to phi

beats.narration_text: Why does spin one-half exist when orbital angular momentum forbids it? The algebra allows all half-integers. Orbital angular momentum adds one constraint — single-valuedness of its wave function on the sphere — that rules out half-integers. Spin lives in an abstract space with no sphere, so no constraint applies.

card.copy: Algebra: all half-integers allowed.  Orbital: sphere adds e^{2πim}=1 → integers only.  Spin: no sphere, no constraint.
